verdicts.title: get_option('duplicate_post_blacklist') flows into array_map('trim') - claimed RCE
verdicts.attack_vector: The scanner flagged array_map('trim', $meta_blacklist) as a dangerous sink, but array_map with the callback 'trim' is not a dangerous function. The option value is read in duplicate_post_plugin_upgrade() (admin-functions.php:162-172), split by comma, trimmed, and then used purely for in_array() membership checks and written back to the database via update_option(). There is no shell execution, no file inclusion, no SQL query, and no eval anywhere in this flow. The 'cmd' and 'path' labels are entirely phantom. This function only runs from admin_init hook (line 39) and already requires is_admin() (line 9 guard) plus the plugin upgrade path (line 86 check). There is no security impact.
verdicts.auth_required: admin (admin_init hook, is_admin() guard at top of file)
verdicts.remediation: No remediation required. The scanner incorrectly classified array_map('trim') as a dangerous sink.
verdicts.false_positive_reason: The scanner labeled array_map('trim', ...) as a code-execution sink, which it is not. This callback is the built-in PHP string function 'trim'. There is no path from this option value to any shell, file-include, eval, or SQL sink.

verdicts.title: $_GET['post'] flows through get_post() to esc_url(build_new_draft_link()) - claimed XSS
verdicts.attack_vector: In add_new_draft_post_button() (classic-editor.php:132-149), $_GET['post'] is sanitized with intval() immediately at line 135, making it a safe integer. That integer is passed to get_post() which performs a database lookup and returns a WP_Post object. The link builder (link-builder.php:69-96) builds a URL using $post->ID (an integer from the DB) via admin_url() and wp_nonce_url(), then the full URL is output through esc_url(). At no point does attacker-controlled string data reach HTML output unsanitized. The 'taint' through get_post() is a false taint propagation — the scanner incorrectly carries taints from the integer $id through the database-returned object.
verdicts.auth_required: admin (post_submitbox_start hook only fires in wp-admin, is_admin() contexts; permissions_helper->should_links_be_displayed() requires current_user_can('copy_posts'))
verdicts.evidence:
$id   = \intval( \wp_unslash( $_GET['post'] ) );  // sanitized to int
$post = \get_post( $id );                            // DB lookup, returns WP_Post
...
echo \esc_url( $this->link_builder->build_new_draft_link( $post ) );  // esc_url applied
// link_builder uses $post->ID (int) in URL construction
verdicts.remediation: No remediation required. Input is sanitized with intval(), data is fetched from the database by safe integer ID, and output is protected with esc_url().
verdicts.false_positive_reason: Three-layer protection: intval() on input, database normalization via get_post(), and esc_url() on output. The scanner incorrectly propagated XSS taint through a get_post() DB call and treated esc_url() as an insufficient sanitizer.

verdicts.title: $_GET['post'] flows through get_post() to esc_url(build_rewrite_and_republish_link()) - claimed XSS
verdicts.attack_vector: Identical pattern to duplicate-post-xss-001 in add_rewrite_and_republish_post_button() (classic-editor.php:158-178). $_GET['post'] is sanitized with intval() at line 161, get_post() returns a DB-sourced WP_Post, and the URL is output through esc_url(). No unsanitized attacker-controlled data reaches HTML output.
verdicts.auth_required: admin (post_submitbox_start hook, requires copy_posts capability via should_links_be_displayed())
verdicts.evidence:
$id   = \intval( \wp_unslash( $_GET['post'] ) );  // sanitized to int
$post = \get_post( $id );                            // DB lookup
...
echo \esc_url( $this->link_builder->build_rewrite_and_republish_link( $post ) );  // esc_url applied
verdicts.remediation: No remediation required. Same analysis as xss-001 applies.
verdicts.false_positive_reason: Same false taint propagation as xss-001: intval() + DB normalization + esc_url() provide complete protection.

verdicts.title: $_GET['post'] flows through get_post() to esc_url(build_check_link()) - claimed XSS; unquoted href attribute noted
verdicts.attack_vector: In add_check_changes_link() (classic-editor.php:187-206), $_GET['post'] is sanitized with intval() at line 190. The same three-layer protection applies: intval() → get_post() → esc_url(). Additionally, the function requires is_rewrite_and_republish_copy($post) to return true, meaning the post must have the '_dp_is_rewrite_republish_copy' meta set to 1 in the database — an attacker cannot influence this via GET parameter manipulation. One cosmetic HTML issue was observed: the href attribute at line 200 lacks surrounding double-quotes (href=<?php echo esc_url(...) ?>>). However, esc_url() strips spaces, angle brackets, and special characters from URLs, preventing attribute breakout even without quotes. Furthermore this runs in wp-admin only for authenticated users with copy_posts capability. No exploitable vulnerability exists.
verdicts.auth_required: admin (post_submitbox_misc_actions hook, requires is_rewrite_and_republish_copy() = true which is a DB-stored meta value)
verdicts.remediation: No security remediation required. As a code quality improvement, add quotes around the href attribute value at line 200: href="<?php echo esc_url(...); ?>" for standards compliance.
verdicts.false_positive_reason: Same false taint propagation as xss-001/002. The additional gate (is_rewrite_and_republish_copy checks a DB meta value) makes exploitation even more impossible. The unquoted href is a code style issue only — esc_url() strips characters that would allow attribute injection.

summary: All 4 flagged taint flows are false positives. The scanner made two systematic errors: (1) it classified array_map('trim', ...) as a dangerous code-execution sink in the RCE finding; (2) it propagated XSS taint through intval() sanitization and a database round-trip (get_post()), treating the resulting WP_Post object as tainted when all attacker-controlled input had already been discarded. All three XSS flows further apply esc_url() on output, and all execute only in the WordPress admin behind authentication and capability checks. The duplicate-post plugin (v4.x, Yoast) demonstrates mature security practices: consistent intval() for integer inputs, esc_url()/esc_html_e() at all output points, wp_nonce_url() on action links, and capability checks via the Permissions_Helper class.
feedback: The taint engine needs two fixes: (1) do not treat array_map with a safe built-in callback like 'trim', 'intval', 'sanitize_text_field', etc. as a sink or taint propagator — these are sanitizers; (2) treat integer-cast variables (intval/absint) as sanitized and do not propagate taint through get_post()/get_user() DB lookups where the return value is an ORM object, not a reflection of the attacker string. Additionally, the confidence=high label on all four flows was misleading given these fundamental analysis errors.
